channel.title: Comments for page ""Baby's First Guide to Keter-Class Anomalies" & Other Questionable Documents Recovered from the Hard Drive of Dr. ████ ███████"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread ""Baby's First Guide to Keter-Class Anomalies" & Other Questionable Documents Recovered from the Hard Drive of Dr. ████ ███████"

item.title: Re: 2521
item.content:encoded:
"I MAY LOOK CREEPY,
BUT I AM A LOAD OF FUN
I DO NOT LIKE WORDS TOO MUCH,
BUT I LOVE DRAWINGS AND SUCH

IF YOU EVER WANT TO HANG OUT, JUST GIVE ME A CALL,
AND I WILL COME ON OVER, STRAIGHT THROUGH YOUR WALL
BUT SOMETIMES PEOPLE DO NOT LIKE THAT, SO, ALAS,
THEY HAVE TAKEN TO CALLING ME KETER-CLASS"
Does anyone know what the morse code says?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …..I guess he was hoping for his kids to grow up in his footsteps….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is hard to read if you've read the story

O O F (actual sound of my feels)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really well written. Great job, author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The presentation is cute

But reading that poetry hurt my soul

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Objection: 682 is totally a wizard.

This is so glorious. +1'd easily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In response to your blackboxing, I have a prequel in my sandbox, but it's just a comedy tale rather than something with images/fun gimmicky stuff. I'm also waiting for [REDACTED] and I to move forward with a larger project in which it'll be included rather than just posting it on its own, but he has been rather busy lately. Nonetheless, there'll be more of this storyline somehow/sometime. I'm glad you like it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes.

Yes. This works beautifully. Will you expand on it? Add more items? I hope so, because it was a very neat read. I'm liking your work, a whole lot!

(A note on how the Doctor is not married nor is expecting a child would be delicious.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The narration was pretty well done…

And then the morse code scared the absolute shit out of me (this was mostly due to the fact that I had headphones on at full volume and due to the fact that I did not heed your comment regarding the morse code, so I was really unprepared for what transpired).

But seriously, the narration was quite good and congratulations on +100!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey hey! Because I have no life, I did a reading of this! If you want to hear my awful, godforsaken, all-hopes-lost squeaky nerd voice reading you bedtimes stories and cutting off abruptly to make way for loud beeping and morse code, give it a listen!

disclaimer: it was recorded in Adobe Audition with my headset mic and is thus not top-notch

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did type it by hand (because I hadn't seen that post, and because it's a little bit fun to do things "the long way"), but it was under 5 minutes, because I'm a fast typist. Then it was about 2 minutes correcting typos, because there were some obvious wrong bits in the first translation I got.

I don't think it's that bad, but I do think that the image should probably have used a bit of a larger font size or different font, and avoided the blue-purple streak that runs through the green in the middle of the image. It's pretty clear to me right now, but for some reason it was hard to see when I was transcribing.

(Alternatively, if you want to simplify things for funny people like me, just have a note near the end of the post that transcribes that part in a copy-pastable form!)

…and Anborough delivers vote +100.

Congrats!

item.title: Re: Almost there...
item.content:encoded:
It looks fantastic!

Also that +99 is activating my OCD.

Hooray for +100! :D

item.title: Re: Almost there...
item.content:encoded: Drawing the book and later Photoshopping it took… a good five or six hours total. When I decided I wanted it on the mainsite, I whipped up the little bits of story for it in, like, thirty minutes. The book was the real work.

item.title: Re: Almost there...
item.content:encoded: This was an awesome read. How long did it take you to make this?

item.title: Re: Almost there...
item.content:encoded: augh baby give it to me Seriously, though… What's awesome is that, under my old Wikidot account, I had a good seven or eight tales up over the course of a year and two months or something, and this got more upvotes (now that I think about it, more votes overall) in a few weeks than all of those combined ever did. I'm so happy.

item.title: Almost there...
item.content:encoded: So close to +100

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Had to upvote because you lampshaded the bad poetry.

I was glad to see that at the end as well. But really, it also works because so many children's books actually do have terrible rhyming schemes and meter. It pisses me off to an irrational degree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man, did you type that all into a translator? I hope you saw this post, because otherwise that's a lot of work. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A transcription of the 2521 entry:

 ../-- .- -.--/.-.. --- --- -.-/-.-. .-. . . .--. -.-- --..-- -... ..- -/../.- -- / .- / .-.. --- .- -.. / --- ..-. / ..-. ..- -. .. / -.. --- / -. --- - / .-.. .. -.- . / .-- --- .-. -.. ... / - --- --- / -- ..- -.-. .... --..-- -... ..- - / .. / .-.. --- ...- . / -.. .-. .- .-- .. -. --. .../ .- -. -.. / ... ..- -.-. .... .. ..-. / -.-- --- ..- / . ...- . .-. / .-- .- -. - / - --- / .... .- -. --. / --- ..- - --..-- / .--- ..- ... - / --. .. ...- . / -- . / .- / -.-. .- .-.. .-.. --..-- .- -. -.. / .. / .-- .. .-.. .-.. / -.-. --- -- . / --- -. / --- ...- . .-. --..-- / ... - .-. .- .. --. .... - / - .... .-. --- ..- --. .... / -.-- --- ..- .-. / .-- .- .-.. .-.. -... ..- - / ... --- -- . - .. -- . ... / .--. . --- .--. .-.. . / -.. --- / -. --- - / .-.. .. -.- . / - .... .- - --..--/ ... --- --..-- / .- .-.. .- ... --..-- - .... . -.-- / .... .- ...- . / - .- -.- . -. / - --- / -.-. .- .-.. .-.. .. -. --. / -- . / -.- . - . .-. -....- -.-. .-.. .- ... ...
+ Plaintext
- Hide Plaintext

I MAY LOOK CREEPY,
BUT I AM A LOAD OF FUN
I DO NOT LIKE WORDS TOO MUCH,
BUT I LOVE DRAWINGS AND SUCH

IF YOU EVER WANT TO HANG OUT, JUST GIVE ME A CALL,
AND I WILL COME ON OVER, STRAIGHT THROUGH YOUR WALL
BUT SOMETIMES PEOPLE DO NOT LIKE THAT, SO, ALAS,
THEY HAVE TAKEN TO CALLING ME KETER-CLASS

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, that was fantastic. I know you said this wasn't intended for SCP-231, but it just gets so much more awesome imagining that it is. Still, the 682 bit had soda coming out of my nose. Good job!

item.title: Re: Fixing the poetry
item.content:encoded:
Still, if you're interested in maybe making a second edition of the actual book, I'm willing to help.

Thank you! I'll keep it in mind. Right now it's just my friend with the child that has a physical copy, but if I ever do anything else with it, I'll PM you. I appreciate it.

item.title: Re: Fixing the poetry
item.content:encoded: That's totally reasonable about not wanting to edit the article. Still, if you're interested in maybe making a second edition of the actual book, I'm willing to help.

item.title: Re: Fixing the poetry
item.content:encoded: Well… I don't know. That amount of editing would make this a collab, and it's already so highly rated that I don't really want to change anything (not to mention editing the Photoshop document, moving all the text around to accommodate new spacing, etc.), especially because I threw in a joke about the bad poetry by the actual character in the story. I do appreciate the offer, though, because poetry is not my strong suit by any means.

item.title: Fixing the poetry
item.content:encoded:
So, you mentioned that the poetry isn't that great. Let's fix that. You seem to be going for a simple AABB rhyme scheme, which is good for a kids' poem. Your meter is pretty close to anapestic tetrameter, which is also good for kids poems (Dr. Seuss used it all the time). Said meter also happens to work well with a lot of SCP designations if you pronounce them as "ESS cee pee SIX eighty-TWO". So, attempting to fit what you've written into that, I get this for the first page.

Hi there, I'm SCP-682
I'm big and I'm fierce and I'm strong just like you!
I'm hard to destroy, and I'm likely to scare;
I'm ugly and mean and I'm covered in hair!

Now some things are magic, religious, or memes,
but I'm just a lizard that's more than he seems!
I'm angry and grumpy and I'm full of sass,
and that is the reason that I'm Keter-class!

I can do the others if you're interested, although 2512 will be difficult for several reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Problematic age? 2422-C will still grow, I think *Lenny face*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If the researcher disagrees with the Foundation's actions as a result of witnessing them, can he really be held responsible for what other employees did to her independently of his involvement?

He is still responsible for his own choices, and under the circumstances I don't think choosing to have sex with a humanoid object in containment (or a person denied freedom and put in circumstances that impair judgment) is an ethical one. Maybe he is just really naive, but I don't think naivete is a defense.

Anyway, that's all I probably am going to say on this. As you say it is getting off topic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you take the Foundation out of this, and imagined if the researcher had done these things independently, could she really be said to have consented to sex?

Alright, now it really is coming down to headcanon. If the researcher disagrees with the Foundation's actions as a result of witnessing them, can he really be held responsible for what other employees did to her independently of his involvement? (Not to mention we're talking about things that aren't even in the article itself, here.) His position as a Foundation employee doesn't mean he is the Foundation. As stated in my earlier comment, he disagrees with the Foundation's actions at this point, and as implied in the article, he wants to get her out. And for him to make that book for her/the baby, and throw his career out the window/risk punishment by the Foundation himself, he must care pretty extensively about her.

But what we're talking about now is how we view the character himself, and as the author, I feel it necessary to make clear the fact that I did not write nor intend to write a rapist. (And because this is getting off-topic now, I'll refrain from making any more replies.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… you're saying that because something bad could have happened, the entire situation is bad because of possibility only?

She was kidnapped, probably extensively drugged, dehumanized, and experimented on. If you take the Foundation out of this, and imagined if the researcher had done these things independently, could she really be said to have consented to sex?

Usually if someone is kidnapped, imprisoned, dehumanized and drugged, and then has sex with the captor, it is a really serious criminal act. Right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure she could genuinely have chosen to have sex with the researcher, but the researcher has all the power here and could also have coerced her, and really because of the huge power imbalance even very subtle (even subconscious) coercion can be effective.

So… you're saying that because something bad could have happened, the entire situation is bad because of possibility only?

I respect your opinion on it, and I'm comfortable dropping the subject here, as long as anyone reading this knows that this is a matter of headcanon only and what I'm implying as the author (combined with my personal understanding of the Foundation universe and power discrepancies therein) is not this. So we're good here, on my end at least. Any and all art and writing comes down to interpretation, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't say that consent was impossible, I said that it was impossible to establish consent. Sure she could genuinely have chosen to have sex with the researcher, but the researcher has all the power here and could also have coerced her, and really because of the huge power imbalance even very subtle (even subconscious) coercion can be effective. She wants to escape, and getting pregnant is possibly a means to that end (which in your narrative it even was). There simply can't be equality in this relationship. For the researcher to enter in to it, knowing this, is unethical.

The Foundation has denied her freedom and even her personhood. They can pump her full of psychoactive drugs, disrupt her environment and erode her self awareness, starve her, perform invasive surgery, artificially inseminate her or even kill her. There are examples of these kind of things all through the archive. How can she possibly be held to have affirmative consent under these conditions.

Like I said, I will prefer to imagine she was pregnant before containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, but it is!

(For properly defined parameters of sanity. Really, my mind is a heavily walled fortress of calm and stability, that greatly enjoys inviting the eldritch horrors around for tea. Care for a spot?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: WJS, nothing here is safe for your sanity…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my earlier drafts of SCP-2203 included a researcher that was paired with an SCP, just to drive home the idea that just because they're perfect together doesn't mean it's still a good idea.

But really, yes, there are plenty of possibilities for other SCPs that become pregnant. I could easily see SCP-2288 do it out of mayhem. SCP-347 has been talked about from time to time. One of the SCP-2085 catgirls, perhaps? It wouldn't be too hard to imagine someone inadvertently getting feelings for SCP-2422-C either (despite the problematic age).

Really, there are plenty of choices.

(And don't worry, Cyantreuse, these links are safe for your sanity.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is probably impossible to establish consent.

I'm not sure I quite want to get involved in this discussion, but are you saying that because she's a skip and because the Foundation overall dehumanizes her, she's not capable of falling in love? She's a sapient human capable of making her own decisions, no matter who she is or where she is. What if he's unhappy with his job and decides that the ethics of keeping someone in containment are wrong, and love and sex happen to play a part?

This is why prison guards having sex with inmates is a criminal offense.

Inmates actually did something wrong (which is why it's far more likely that a guard would be taking advantage of an inmate), whereas skips are just imprisoned because they're abnormal. She can easily give consent if it's a mutually respectful relationship, which goes back to the whole him-realizing-what-the-Foundation-does-is-wrong thing.

If you want to argue Stockholm Syndrome and related factors, then that's another case, but it's not what I was envisioning when I wrote this and I'm actually quite bothered to see it interpreted as anything other than a consensual relationship. If anyone else interpreted it like this, please let me know. That's not something I want implied on the mainsite. This article is supposed to be cute and lighthearted in all its elements, not just the book part, and I apologize if there's moral confusion here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
he and the skip he was assigned to fell in love and she got pregnant, so it's just the typical "oh crap I'm a dad now what do I do"

This is itself problematic. The researcher (especially at the Foundation where normal ethics don't apply) has all the power in this relationship. It is probably impossible to establish consent. This is why prison guards having sex with inmates is a criminal offense. I think I liked this better when I imagined the woman was already pregnant…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, that's some impressive theorization, and I know 231 is the default image for a pregnant skip, but remember that a lot of humanoids are human females perfectly capable of getting pregnant normally. While the reader can interpret it however they want regardless, the only thing I was trying to convey was that he and the skip he was assigned to fell in love and she got pregnant, so it's just the typical "oh crap I'm a dad now what do I do" except in a setting where it's massively against the rules and now they need to ~*run away together*~1.

As for which skip it is, it doesn't really matter. It could be 2156, but she'd be a massive pain in the ass to take care of. Could be Iris, or Lilith, or anyone else. (Not saying it's one of those listed, just giving examples of skips that are relatively biologically normal.)

P.S.: I don't think a baby will care about the poetry, it rhyming is far enough, and also, if you mind me giving you advice… you need to get this published as a real book, I'd totally buy and I bet many others would.

Can't because of 173 being in there. If I took him out, then maybe. I already laminated the original copy and gave it to my friend, which was all I really wanted to do, but who knows? I'm just glad you like it. :D

Footnotes
1. *big pink sparkly hearts*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What if Dr. ████ ███████ was assigned to SCP-231-7 and not only felt sympathy towards her, but he also fell in love with her? What if

once we're out of here and the baby's out of you! Which we will be! Out of here, I mean! Very soon! Just sit tight down there while I get the last of everything straightened out

refers to him being a researcher, and her being an SCP, and his plans to free her for containment? And what if he tried and got terminated and now they're examinating his files to see if he had done more wrong to the Foundation? There you go, it's adorable and sweet that you did it for your friend, and the story was too, but now is also romantic. +1 without thinking too much.

P.S.: I don't think a baby will care about the poetry, it rhyming is far enough, and also, if you mind me giving you advice… you need to get this published as a real book, I'd totally buy and I bet many others would.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just wish that you had made this more conceptually distinct from 009-J.

Well, I certainly respect your vote, but I have to disagree with you there. 009-J is simply real-life pictures with one-or-two-sentence captions. I hand-drew this and Photoshopped it, wrote the (admittedly badly written, but original and time-consuming nonetheless) poems, and gave it the theme of Keter-class skips in particular rather than just the Foundationverse overall/the "where's the [thing]" kiddy book trope. With the addition of a short story that adds context to the end page in particular, I can say I'm comfortable differentiating this from 009-J. After all, this isn't a joke page - it just happens to have a cute theme.

(Edit: In case this doesn't make sense, I mean that I feel the criteria above bring enough new content to the table to make up for the lack of conceptual difference.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cute, and kudos on a unique and creative presentation. I just wish that you had made this more conceptually distinct from 009-J. Unfortunately, this doesn't do enough to distinguish itself from that piece, so -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I keep seeing the title and thinking about all of the many, many things that are wrong with that particular combination of words. (In a good way, mind you.) X3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Color printer, laminator or plastic sleeves, and a binder. Quick and easy nonprofessional kids' book.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that's what I meant. Silly me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I thought of that when I was making this, but… 009-J's just real-life pictures with captions rather than hand-drawn images and multiple lines. :/ (Which is probably what killerax meant in the original post.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suspect the Thekillerax meant SCP-009-J, which is also a an SCP themed children's book.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cripes, the pregnant ones seem to be the darkest articles.

Also, as far as the three-box thing,

digression!
– hide block

For one I kinda assumed it might be an SCP he had previous exposure to, maybe before the transfer, and for two I have it slightly headcanoned that they would add a 0 to the front of Series Is before blackboxing SCPs, because giving them a digit count is sloppy redaction, reducing the options by 1/3 or 2/3 (considering current series only).

Belated upvote with the morse translation :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is it similar?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Easy +1 dude. If the SCP starts publishing books I would buy this for my kids, if I have any.

I do got a few baby cousins though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's (personified/animate/smiley-faced) 447-1 and the 'blanket' is 447-2. Which probably shouldn't have had black outlines or whatever I did. (Has nothing to do with anything; I just like 447 so I threw it in there when I made this for its original purposes.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had to upvote because you lampshaded the bad poetry. :V No, but this is great. What's that smiling pea under the blanket on the final page?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My new headcanon is what Petrograd said, combined with the implications of Fear Alone by djkaktus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is adorable and sweet, and it made me smile, so easy +1. And the fact you actually made this for a friend just makes it even better, I think…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was originally recommending SCP-2401.

Although, if we don't really know how the physiology works, SCP-2662 would be a fun choice.

ETA: *smirk*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh lord, no. This isn't nearly that dark. Also, the skip he's assigned to has four blackboxes, so it's four digits. As for the morse, see my reply to LurkD up there.

Edit: Auugghh WJS, why do you insist on regularly plaguing my life with references to that accursed article? (2401, not… not the other one.) That first image is burned into my mind enough as it is, go easy…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here's the original image from my Tumblr post/the original version of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can anyone translate the morse? I'm on my phone and don't have an easy way to pull it from the image.

As for the recipient, I have a really sinking feeling it's SCP-231.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didnt identify it as morse. Good to know though. What does it say?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going to upvote just with 682 (that big lizard sure is full of sass) and then it just got better and better. And that last note was heartwarming enough to make the context belivable.

Take your upboat you genius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The SCP-2521 bit sealed my upvote. It's a bit similar to SCP-006-J, but the cute images and format are enough to differentiate the two for me.
